schools.summary: Paul refers back to the glorious ministry of the new covenant just described (ch. 3); 'having this ministry' — so high and honourable a trust — is the ground of his perseverance; and he ascribes his having it to God's mercy, not his own merit (he, the former persecutor, made a minister by sheer grace, 1 Timothy 1:13), so that 'we faint not' (lose heart, grow weary) amid its trials (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry, Chrysostom): because the ministry is so glorious and was given by mercy, Paul does not faint or give up amid its trials — grace both bestows the office and sustains the minister.

themes: seeing we have this ministry, as we have received mercy, we faint not
Paul's perseverance in the ministry
the ministry received by mercy
steadfastness grounded in grace

schools.summary: Paul has 'renounced' (cast off) 'the hidden things of dishonesty (shame)' — secret, shameful practices; he uses no concealed, underhanded methods (unlike the false teachers), does not walk in 'craftiness' (cunning) nor 'handle the word of God deceitfully' (adulterate, falsify, corrupt the Scriptures for gain or to please men), but commends himself by the open 'manifestation of the truth' to every man's conscience, in the sight of God (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): Paul keeps the gospel pure, neither corrupting it nor using trickery; he appeals to conscience and acts always before God — the mark of a faithful, transparent ministry.

themes: renounced the hidden things of dishonesty, not walking in craftiness, nor handling the word of God deceitfully
by manifestation of the truth commending ourselves to every man's conscience
Paul's renunciation of deceit
open dealing with the truth

schools.summary: Paul answers an objection: if his gospel (so clear and open, v.2) is 'hid' (veiled, obscure) to some, it is hid only 'to them that are lost' (the perishing, those on the way to ruin) — not from any defect in the gospel; the fault lies with the hearers, not the message, the lost unable to see the plain gospel because of their own perishing condition (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the clarity of the gospel is not diminished by the blindness of those who reject it; if any do not see it, it is because they are perishing.

themes: if our gospel be hid, it is hid to them that are lost
the gospel hidden only to the lost
the obscurity in the hearer, not the message
the clarity of the gospel

schools.summary: 'The god of this world' is Satan — so called not by essence (he has no divine attributes) but because the wicked world worships and obeys him as its god, having his homage as idols do (the prince of this world, John 12:31; the prince of the power of the air, Ephesians 2:2); he blinds the minds of unbelievers, lest the light of the gospel of Christ — 'the image of God,' the exact representation of his nature and glory (Colossians 1:15; Hebrews 1:3) — shine on them (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Calvin: no one judging rightly can doubt it is Satan; he is called 'god' as Baal is the god of his worshippers, by usurped dominion over unbelievers — this gives no support to the Arians (who pressed it against Christ's deity) or the Manicheans (who made him an independent evil principle), for Satan blinds only by God's permission and command, as the minister of his wrath.

Clarke: prefers to read it of the true God, who in judgment gave over the unbelieving to spiritual darkness (Isaiah 6:9; Romans 11:8), re-parsing 'of this world' with 'unbelievers' — the reading of almost all the ancients (Irenaeus, Tertullian, Chrysostom, Theodoret, Augustine), reluctant to give Satan the title 'God.'

debates.question: Is 'the god of this world' Satan or the true God?

sides.position: Satan — the god this wicked world worships and obeys, by usurped dominion (not essence)
The true God, who judicially blinds the unbelievers of this world ('of this world' read with 'unbelievers')

themes: the god of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not
the light of the glorious gospel of Christ, who is the image of God
the blinding of unbelievers' minds
the god of this world crux

schools.summary: Paul's central proclamation: he does not preach 'ourselves' (his own glory, opinions, or party) but 'Christ Jesus the Lord' — Christ as the supreme Lord and Saviour, the sole subject of his preaching; and presents himself not as lord over their faith but as their 'servant (bondslave) for Jesus' sake,' devoting himself to their good for Christ's sake (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): the confession of Christ's lordship — his deity and sovereignty proclaimed; the minister serves the church under Christ, exalting the Lord and not himself.

themes: we preach not ourselves, but Christ Jesus the Lord
ourselves your servants for Jesus' sake
Christ preached as Lord, the minister as servant
the Christ-centred ministry

schools.summary: Paul alludes to creation (Genesis 1:3): the same God who commanded light to shine out of darkness now 'hath shined in our hearts' — a divine, inward illumination, dispelling the darkness of the unregenerate mind, a new creation of light within; the light being 'the knowledge of the glory of God,' seen 'in the face (person) of Jesus Christ,' in whom God's glory is revealed — to know Christ is to see the glory of God (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: God works a new creation in the soul, shining inwardly to give the saving knowledge of his glory in Christ; the light of saving knowledge is God's own gift, not man's attainment.

themes: God, who commanded the light to shine out of darkness, hath shined in our hearts
the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ
the inward shining of God's light
the glory of God in Christ

schools.summary: Paul's striking figure: 'this treasure' (the glorious gospel, the light of the knowledge of God) is held 'in earthen vessels' (fragile, common clay pots) — the apostles, weak, mortal, afflicted men; God's purpose being that the surpassing 'power' (the converting, sustaining power evident in the ministry) might manifestly be 'of God, and not of us,' the weakness of the vessels making clear that the power belongs to God alone (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Cyril): God deliberately entrusts the treasure to weak men, that all the glory of its effects may be ascribed to him, not to the human instruments — the power shining the brighter for the frailty of the vessel.

themes: we have this treasure in earthen vessels
that the excellency of the power may be of God, and not of us
the treasure of the gospel in earthen vessels
God's power through human weakness

schools.summary: Paul describes the apostles' sufferings in a series of contrasts: 'troubled (pressed, afflicted) on every side, yet not distressed (not crushed, not straitened)' — hard pressed but not hemmed in to destruction; 'perplexed (at a loss, not knowing what to do) but not in despair (not utterly without resource or hope)' — a wordplay, at a loss but not at a total loss (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry, Chrysostom): these paradoxes show the divine power sustaining the frail vessels — afflicted yet upheld; the power of God (v.7) displayed in their preservation amid suffering.

themes: troubled on every side, yet not distressed
perplexed, but not in despair
the apostles afflicted yet not overcome
divine power sustaining the afflicted

schools.summary: Paul continues the contrasts: 'persecuted, but not forsaken' (pursued by enemies, yet not abandoned by God); 'cast down, but not destroyed' (struck down, thrown to the ground, yet not killed or made an end of); the language perhaps drawn from the battlefield or the arena — pursued, struck down, but not slain, God's preserving hand evident in each deliverance (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: Persecuted, but not forsaken; cast down, but not destroyed
the apostles persecuted yet not forsaken
God's preserving hand in extremity
not abandoned, not slain

schools.summary: Paul and the apostles 'always' carry about 'the dying (the death-process, constant exposure to death) of the Lord Jesus' — in their bodies they share Christ's sufferings, bearing the likeness of his death; the design being that 'the life of Jesus' (his risen, sustaining life and power) might be displayed in their bodies, their preservation amid deadly perils manifesting the living power of the risen Christ (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: as the apostles share Christ's death in their sufferings, so they share his life in their deliverances; the dying and the rising of Jesus are both displayed in them.

themes: bearing about in the body the dying of the Lord Jesus
that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our body
bearing Christ's dying that his life be shown
the pattern of death and resurrection

schools.summary: Paul repeats and explains v.10: 'we which live' (the living apostles) are 'alway delivered unto death for Jesus' sake' — constantly exposed to deadly peril because of their service to Christ; the design being that the life (the sustaining power) of Jesus might be displayed 'in our mortal flesh,' the more clearly because the flesh is mortal and frail — the survival of dying men manifesting the power of the living Christ (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: the very mortality of their flesh, kept alive amid constant danger, displays the immortal life and power of Jesus.

themes: we which live are alway delivered unto death for Jesus' sake
that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our mortal flesh
delivered to death that Christ's life be shown
the power of Christ's life in mortal flesh

schools.summary: Paul draws the conclusion: 'so then death worketh in us' (the apostles' sufferings and exposure to death) 'but life in you' (the Corinthians) — the apostles' deadly toils and dangers result in the spiritual life and benefit of the church; the gracious exchange by which the apostles endure dying and the church gains life through the gospel they bring (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the apostles' afflictions are not in vain but fruitful — through their dying, life flows to others; a pattern of self-sacrificing ministry for the good of the church.

themes: death worketh in us, but life in you
death in the apostles, life in the church
the fruitful exchange of suffering
self-sacrificing ministry

schools.summary: Paul claims 'the same spirit of faith' as the psalmist — the same believing disposition (wrought by the Spirit) that animated the saints of old; quoting Psalm 116:10, 'I believed, and therefore have I spoken' — faith in the heart issues in confession with the mouth; so the apostles, sustained by faith amid their sufferings, believe and therefore continue to preach (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin (with Wesley): like the psalmist, the apostles' faith (in the resurrection, v.14) is the spring of their bold testimony; believing, they cannot but speak, even amid suffering.

themes: We having the same spirit of faith
I believed, and therefore have I spoken; we also believe, and therefore speak
the spirit of faith that speaks
faith the spring of testimony

schools.summary: The ground of the apostles' faith and boldness: 'knowing' (with assured confidence) that God, 'who raised up the Lord Jesus, shall raise up us also' — the resurrection of Christ the pledge of the believer's resurrection; 'by (through) Jesus,' by his power and as the firstfruits, and God 'shall present us with you,' bringing the apostles and the Corinthians together into his presence, glorified, at the last day — this certain hope sustaining the apostles amid their dying (Barnes, Ellicott).

themes: he which raised up the Lord Jesus shall raise up us also by Jesus
shall present us with you
the hope of resurrection through Jesus
Christ's resurrection the pledge of ours

schools.summary: Paul affirms that 'all things' — his sufferings, labours, and the whole ministry — are 'for your sakes' (for the benefit of the church), not for himself; the goal being that the 'abundant grace' (spreading to more and more people through the ministry) might, 'through the thanksgiving of many,' 'redound (overflow, abound) to the glory of God' — the more are saved, the more thanksgiving rises, and the more God is glorified (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the chain — the apostles suffer for the church's good, the church's salvation produces thanksgiving, and thanksgiving glorifies God; the whole ministry ordered to God's glory.

themes: all things are for your sakes
that the abundant grace might through the thanksgiving of many redound to the glory of God
all for the church and God's glory
grace, thanksgiving, and glory

schools.summary: Paul repeats his theme (from v.1): 'for which cause' — because of the glory of the ministry and the hope of resurrection — 'we faint not'; though 'the outward man' (the body, the physical frame) is 'perishing' (decaying, wasting away under affliction, age, and toil), 'the inward man' (the soul, the spiritual life, the new nature) 'is renewed day by day' — daily strengthened, refreshed, and growing by grace (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): while the body decays, the believer's spiritual life flourishes — the inward man renewed daily by grace, growing as the body fails.

themes: we faint not; but though our outward man perish, yet the inward man is renewed day by day
the inward man renewed though the outward perishes
spiritual flourishing amid bodily decay
daily renewal by grace

schools.summary: Paul weighs the present sufferings against the coming glory: the affliction, though severe, is 'light' (in comparison) and 'but for a moment' (momentary, transient) — small and brief beside what awaits; against it stands 'a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory,' the contrast at every point (light vs weight, momentary vs eternal, affliction vs glory), 'far more exceeding' (beyond all measure), the coming glory infinitely outweighing the present suffering (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): the affliction 'worketh (produces, achieves)' the glory — not as merit, but as the appointed means and preparation; the trials themselves, by God's grace, work toward the eternal reward.

themes: our light affliction, which is but for a moment
worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory
present affliction working eternal glory
the surpassing recompense

schools.summary: The condition of the foregoing (v.17): the affliction works glory 'while we look not (do not fix our gaze) at the things which are seen (the visible, present, earthly) but at the things which are not seen (the invisible, future, heavenly)' — the eye of faith fixed on the unseen; the ground being that the seen things are 'temporal' (temporary, passing) while the unseen are 'eternal' (everlasting, abiding forever), so the eternal is infinitely more worthy of regard (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: faith looks beyond the visible and transient to the invisible and eternal; this heavenly perspective makes the present afflictions light and the coming glory weighty.

themes: we look not at the things which are seen, but at the things which are not seen
the things which are seen are temporal; but the things which are not seen are eternal
the gaze fixed on the eternal unseen
the heavenly perspective of faith
